coredata.dc:title: Preparation of fluorescent nitrogen-doped carbon dots for highly selective on-off detection of Fe3+ ions in real samples
coredata.dc:creator: Hashemi, Nazanin
Mousazadeh, Mohammad H.
coredata.dc:description: In this work, nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) based fluorescent probes were synthesized using m-phenylenediamine and citric acid as both nitrogen and carbon source through the hydrothermal method. The structural and optical properties of synthesized N-CDs were investigated by transmission electron microscopy (TEM), dynamic light scattering (DLS), X-ray diffraction (XRD), Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR), UV–vis spectroscopy, Zeta potential, and photoluminescence (PL). The water-soluble N-CDs showed a narrow size distribution within 6–8 nm. The emission peak with maximum intensity for N-CDs was 535 nm at the excitation wavelength of 470 nm, and the quantum yield of N-CDs was 30.2%. The as-prepared N-CDs could be selectively quenched by Fe3+ ions with a limit of detection (LOD) of 13.8 nM in a linear range from 0.002 μM to 8 μM. Furthermore, examining the validity of the present fluorescence N-CDs probe showed that the proposed method has enough reliability and sensitivity for detecting Fe3+ ions in an acceptable recovery range from 97 to 106.4% in the real samples. The probe indicated high stability and selectivity to be a suitable candidate for environmental applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dot
Static quenching
Fe3+ ion
Citric acid
m-phenylenediamine

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Characterization and optical properties of N-CDs

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors are interested in acknowledging Amirkabir University of Technology (Tehran, Iran) for this work's financial support.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: a) TEM image of prepared N-CDs, b) DLS analysis of N-CDs, c) XRD spectrum, d) the Zeta potential of N-CDs at pH 6.0, and e) FTIR spectra of the prepared N-CDs.

a) The influence of reaction temperature on the fluorescence intensity of prepared N-CDs, b) the impact of pH on the fluorescence intensity of synthesized N-CDs, c) fluorescence emission intensity of N-CDs under continuous excitation at 470 nm for 100 min, d) fluorescence emission intensity of the N-CDs stored at ambient conditions over time (1, 3, 7, 14, 30, and 60 days), and e) fluorescence emission spectra of N-CDs at different concentrations of NaCl (0–2.5 M).
a) UV–vis absorption and emission spectra of N-CDs, and b) fluorescence emission spectra of prepared N-CDs (10 μM) at different excitation wavelengths from 370 nm to 520 nm.

a) The selectivity of N-CDs (10 μM) in the presence of different metal ions for Fe3+ and Cu2+ detection at pH 6.0, and b) the selectivity of N-CDs (10 μM) in the presence of different metal ions for detection of Fe3+ at pH 3.0. The concentrations of all metal ions were 7 μM.

a) Fluorescence emission spectra of the N-CDs (10 μM) in the presence of different concentrations of Fe3+ (from top to bottom: 0.0, 0.002, 0.005, 0.018, 0.054, 0.179, 0.89, 1.79, 3.58, 7.16 and 8 μM), and b) the linear relationship of F0/F and Fe3+ ion concentrations over the range of 0.002–8 μM with R2 = 0.998. F and F0 are the emission intensity at 535 nm in the presence and absence of Fe3+ ions, respectively.

a) Stern–Volmer plots of N-CDs in the presence of different concentrations of Fe3+ at three different temperatures (298, 303, and 308 K), b) the Zeta potential of N-CDs in the presence and absence of Fe3+ ions, and c) UV–vis absorption of 1. Fe3+ (green line), 2. N-CDs (blue line), and 3. N-CDs/Fe3+ (red line).

The cell viability of HeLa cells after treatment with various concentrations (0–1.4 mg mL−1) of N-CDs for 24 h by MTT method. The standard deviation of three separate experiments was indicated with error bars.
Comparison of the fluorescence emission wavelength and QY of N-CDs with different m-PD and citric acid ratios.
Comparison of present work with different properties of reported works for detection of Fe3+ ions.
The static Stern–Volmer quenching constant at three different temperatures for N-CDs/Fe3+ sensing platform.
Determination of Fe3+ ions in different real water samples.

head.ce:title: Preparation of fluorescent nitrogen-doped carbon dots for highly selective on-off detection of Fe3+ ions in real samples

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Amirkabir University of Technology, 15875-4413, Tehran, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Amirkabir University of Technology, 15875-4413, Tehran, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) based fluorescent probes were synthesized using m-phenylenediamine and citric acid as both nitrogen and carbon source through the hydrothermal method. The structural and optical properties of synthesized N-CDs were investigated by transmission electron microscopy (TEM), dynamic light scattering (DLS), X-ray diffraction (XRD), Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR), UV–vis spectroscopy, Zeta potential, and photoluminescence (PL). The water-soluble N-CDs showed a narrow size distribution within 6–8 nm. The emission peak with maximum intensity for N-CDs was 535 nm at the excitation wavelength of 470 nm, and the quantum yield of N-CDs was 30.2%. The as-prepared N-CDs could be selectively quenched by Fe3+ ions with a limit of detection (LOD) of 13.8 nM in a linear range from 0.002 μM to 8 μM. Furthermore, examining the validity of the present fluorescence N-CDs probe showed that the proposed method has enough reliability and sensitivity for detecting Fe3+ ions in an acceptable recovery range from 97 to 106.4% in the real samples. The probe indicated high stability and selectivity to be a suitable candidate for environmental applications.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, water-soluble carbon dots (CDs) have attracted more attention than other quantum dots types because of their fascinating optical properties in biosensors [1], light-emitting devices [2], drug delivery [3], photocatalysis [4,5], and bioimaging [6]. Semiconductor quantum dots and water-soluble CDs are two kinds of fluorescent-based probes that are widely utilized [7,8]. Unlike CDs, semiconductor quantum dots are highly toxic because of heavy metals presence like Cd, Te, Se, Pb, etc. Therefore, CDs have attracted significant interest due to their superior optical properties, high photostability, ease of fabrication, low toxicity, and low-cost fabrication methods [9,10]. Typically, the design of the CDs-based probe contains coordination of CDs with analytes, post-functionalization of CDs with particular receptors, and integration of CDs with other quenchers or substrates [11]. The modification of surface and the post-functionalization of CDs are among the techniques making CDs suitable for sensing applications [12]. In most studies, blue-emitting CDs are not favored for bioimaging because of the autofluorescence of cells in the blue spectrum region. Consequently, developing the ways to shift the PL emission of CDs to longer wavelength areas is the major significance in this work. Green-emitting CDs are typically synthesized by selecting appropriate precursors with aromatic rings and multiple heteroatoms in the controlled preparation conditions, such as pH, temperature, and time [13].

Detection of Fe3+ in aqueous solutions is critical in biological systems and surrounding environments because it is one of the vital metals in the human body and plays a substantial role in the large numbers of enzymatic reactions. Hence, the improvement of chemosensors for detecting Fe3+ ions is remarkable [13]. Analytical techniques including voltammetric method [14], fiber-optic chemosensor [15], atomic absorption spectroscopy [16], inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP-MS) [17], colorimetric method [18] and chemiluminescent method [19,20] have been widely developed for Fe3+ detection. Drawbacks of these techniques are the necessity of costly tools, time-consuming pre-treatments, and tedious methods. In this regard, fluorescent probes have been advanced in recent years to detect Fe3+ ions. Shamsipour et al. synthesized CDs by the hydrothermal method using p-aminosalicylic acid (PAS) as both carbon and nitrogen sources. The synthesized CDs indicated an excellent affinity for Fe3+ with a reasonable limit of detection (LOD) of 82 nM [13]. Pu et al. synthesized fluorescent CDs by a one-step hydrothermal method in the presence of citric acid and phenylalanine as the precursors. The blue light emission CDs could be quenched in the presence of Fe3+ with a static quenching mechanism [21]. Chen et al. prepared a sensitive fluorescent probe based on a microwave-assisted method, using glutamic acid and ethylenediamine as precursors for detecting Fe3+ ions in an aqueous solution in the range of 8–80 μM, with the optimum emission wavelength at 459 nm under an excitation wavelength of 360 nm [22]. Issa et al. prepared highly fluorescent CDs from carboxymethylcellulose in the presence of linear polyethyleneimine to detect Fe3+ ions in an acidic environment. The prepared CDs were quenched with a high concentration of Fe3+ ions with an acceptable LOD (0.14 μM) [23]. Although these achievements have pioneered the Fe3+ ions tracing, more sensitive fluorescent probes need to be developed.

This study reported the on-off detection of Fe3+ ions in acidic conditions using nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs). The N-CDs were prepared by the hydrothermal method from citric acid and m-phenylenediamine (m-PD) as carbon and nitrogen sources containing aromatic rings and amino groups. The fluorescence N-CDs had strong green light emission around 535 nm, with an excitation wavelength at 470 nm. Detection of Fe3+ was based on the static quenching of the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs in acidic media. The as-prepared N-CDs could be selectively quenched with Fe3+ ions with a LOD of 13.8 nM. The role of amino groups and aromatic rings in m-PD is crucial because they have resulted in green-emitting CDs with a PL quantum yield (QY) of 30.2%. Therefore, the N-CDs are used for highly selective detection of Fe3+ ions in the real samples.

All metal salts were of analytical grade, including Fe2(NO3)3.9H2O, Zn(NO3)2, Ca(NO3)2.4H2O, Ni(NO3).6H2O, As(Cl)3, Pb(NO3)2, Cu(NO3)2.3H2O, Hg(NO3)2.H2O, Rh(NO3).H2O, Pt(NO3)4, Cr(NO3). 9H2O, Ag(NO3), Cd(NO3)2.4H2O, SeO2, Mn(NO3)2.4H2O, (NH₄)₆Mo₇O₂₄, Co(NO3)2.6H2O, Al(NO3)3.9H2O, Pd(NO3)2.2H2O, were obtained from Merck. The citric acid powder and m-phenylenediamine were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. NaCl powder was supplied by Merck. The dialysis membrane (MWCO of 3500 Da) was purchased from co-chemicals (Iran). Rhodamine 6G as a fluorescence standard reference (98%) was obtained from Merck. Deionized water was used in all experiments.

Fluorescent spectra were carried out in quartz cuvette cells using PerkinElmer LS55. UV–vis spectroscopy was performed by a PerkinElmer lambda 45. FTIR spectroscopy was measured with a Bruker alpha and KBr as a standard. XRD analyses were recorded with a Radiation: 1.5406 Cu, Generator: 40 KV, 40 mA, Imax = 1529. The CDs morphology was measured by a JEM-2010 FEF transmission electron microscope (JEOL, Japan)), and Zeta potential was obtained from a Cordouan Tech (WALLIS).

The fluorescent N-CDs were prepared by a one-step hydrothermal method as described in detail previously [24,25]. First, different ratios of citric acid as a carbon source and m-phenylenediamine as a nitrogen source were dissolved into 90 mL deionized water and stirred for 15 min. The resulting solution was transferred into a 200 mL Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave and heated at 200 °C for 10 h. The autoclave and products were then cooled to room temperature naturally. The solution with dark brown precipitations was centrifuged (12000 rpm, 20 min) and filtered through a 0.22 μm filter membrane to collect the filtrant containing N-CDs. Finally, the resulting solution was dialyzed in a dialysis bag (3.5 kDa) for three days to remove impurities and reach N-CDs products.

The QY of synthesized N-CDs is calculated through the following equation [26,27]:
(1)
Q
Y S
= Q
Y R
(
A R
A S
)
(
I S
I R
)
(
N S 2
N R 2
)

R and S refer to the standard reference and N-CDs samples, respectively. The QY is quantum yield, I is integrated PL spectra, A is the absorption, and N is the solvent's refractive index (water, 1.33). The absorbance of the sample was kept below 0.1 at the excitation wavelength of 470 nm. Rhodamine 6G in water with the known QY of 0.95 was selected as a standard reference.

To measure Fe3+ ions in the real samples, tap and mineral waters were used as natural sources. Mineral waters were purchased from the local supermarket, and tap waters were collected from urban water piping. Before analysis, all samples were filtered through (0.45 μM) microporous membranes to collect colloidal particles. Then different concentrations of Fe3+ ions were added to the solution, including the real sample and N-CDs probe. Eventually, the fluorescence emission spectra were recorded at the excitation wavelength (470 nm) for all samples. For all experiments, 500 μL of N-CDs and 200 mL of water samples were mixed.

The cell viability of N-CDs was investigated by MTT assay. In general, the HeLa cells were grown into a 96-well cell culture plate in Dulbecco's Modified Eagle's medium (DMEM) with 10% FBS, 1% penicillin, and streptomycin cultured in an incubator at 37 °C under 5% CO2 for 24 h. The cells were then replaced with DMEM containing different N-CDs concentrations (0–1.4 mg mL−1) and incubated for 24 h at 37 °C with 5% CO2. Afterward, the cells were washed with PBS buffer (20 mL, pH 7.4), and the MTT salt solution was added to each cell and incubated for 5 h (5 mg mL−1 in PBS). Subsequently, the culture medium was aspirated by MTT, and 150 μL of dimethylsulfoxide (DMSO) was added to wells and shaken for 5 min at room temperature. The mixture's absorbance was measured with a microplate reader (ELx800, Biotek, USA) at 490 nm. Furthermore, the control experiments were done without N-CDs at the mentioned conditions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization and optical properties of N-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescence N-CDs with high QY were synthesized with citric acid as a carbon source and m-phenylenediamine as a nitrogen source by a one-step hydrothermal method. The size and morphology of N-CDs have been determined by TEM and dynamic light scattering (DLS) analysis. The TEM image in (Fig. 1 a) demonstrates that as-prepared N-CDs have a spherical shape and an average diameter of 6–8 nm. As shown in (Fig. 1b), the average particle size of N-CDs by DLS is estimated as 10–12 nm. The size of N-CDs measured by TEM is smaller than that achieved by DLS analysis because DLS defines the total hydrodynamic size of ions and adsorbed molecules [13]. As illustrated in (Fig. 1c), the XRD pattern shows a peak at around 2θ = 20.0°, corresponding to (002) graphitic carbon diffraction profiles. The observations exhibit a graphitic nature with many disorganized carbon atoms. N-CDs have higher inter-layer spacing than graphite due to the more functional group's presence on their surface [28]. The Zeta potential is the best indicator for determining the particle's electrical charge because it indicates the accumulation of charge on the surface. The Zeta potential was measured for N-CDs and found to be approximately −10 mV at pH 6.0 (Fig. 1d). The result displays the negatively charged surface of N-CDs due to carboxylic and hydroxyl functional groups presented on the surface of N-CDs. As seen in (Fig. 1e), the FTIR spectrum of the N-CDs indicates the following peaks, stretching vibrations of O–H and N–H at 3468 cm−1, CO stretching vibrations of carbonyl groups at 1689 cm−1, and stretching vibrations of C–O at 1467 cm−1. The peaks at 2145 cm−1 and 730 cm−1 were demonstrated the C–H stretching and bending vibrations, respectively. Also, the peak at 1270 cm−1 corresponds to C–N groups. These functional groups are common in such N-CDs and improve the water solubility and photostability without any extra surface modification [29].

The influence of different reaction temperatures (160, 180, 200, 220 °C) with a reaction time of 10 h at photoluminescent spectra of N-CDs was demonstrated in (Fig. 2 a). The fluorescence intensity of prepared N-CDs at the emission wavelength of 535 nm gradually increased by temperature increasing from 160 °C to 200 °C. As a result, the temperature of 200 °C with a reaction time of 10 h was chosen as the optimum conditions for N-CDs synthesis. A sufficient reaction temperature is necessary to improve PL behavior. When the temperature increases, N-CDs are formed by condensation reactions of dehydration of polymers, and then the polymers are carbonized into the CDs [30]. The effect of pH and ionic strength (based on NaCl concentration) on the fluorescence stability of the as-prepared N-CDs were studied. As illustrated in (Fig. 2b), the optimum pH value for the best efficiency and high fluorescence intensity of as-prepared N-CDs was obtained at pH 6.0, indicated that the formed N-CDs would have the maximum potential for cellular and bioimaging applications [31]. The PL intensity was recorded to measure the photostability of N-CDs under 100 min continuous illumination and 60 days long-term storage at ambient conditions under excitation wavelength at 470 nm. As shown in (Fig. 2c and d), there was no significant change after continuous illumination and long-term storage, distinctly manifesting the high stability of synthesized N-CDs. The ionic strength effect on the PL intensity of N-CDs at different NaCl solution concentrations is shown in (Fig. 2e). The PL intensity remained almost constant with increasing the concentration of NaCl solutions from 0 to 2.5 M, indicating the excellent stability of N-CDs in acutely concentrated salt environments. Also, the ionic strength could decrease N-CDs aggregation due to no ionization of surface functional groups of N-CDs [28]. The UV–vis absorption spectra of the synthesized N-CDs were obtained from series of N-CDs solutions dilutions and indicated in (Fig. 3 a). A sharp peak at around 290 nm occurs due to the π-π* transition of aromatic CC bonds [32]. The synthesized N-CDs showed a strong emission peak at about 535 nm when excited at 470 nm. As shown in (Fig. 3b), N-CDs fluorescence emission spectra were measured at different excitation wavelengths in the range of 370 nm–520 nm. It can be seen that the N-CDs emission intensity showed a maximum intensity at 535 nm with an excitation wavelength at 470 nm. To compare the QY and fluorescence emission wavelengths of N-CDs, different ratios of citric acid/m-PD under the same conditions were used to synthesize N-CDs. As can be seen in (Table 1 ), the QY of sample 3 (ratio 1:2) demonstrated a much stronger fluorescence emission peak at 535 nm compared to other samples. Based on obtained results, the N-CDs exhibited good stability with a sufficient number of functional groups for sensing applications [33].

To evaluate the sensing selectivity of as-prepared N-CDs, the fluorescent intensity was measured in the presence of various metal ions with a concentration of 7 μM under excitation wavelength at 470 nm. According to (Fig. 4 a), the N-CDs emission peak was quenched by Fe3+ and Cu2+ ions. As illustrated in (Fig. 4b), after adjusting the solution pH to 3.0, the fluorescence intensity was quenched only by Fe3+, and no noticeable decrease was observed in fluorescence intensity by other ions. Fe3+and Cu2+ ions illustrated quenching effects at the pH of 6.0. On the other hand, high selectivity for Fe3+ ions was indicated at a pH of 3.0 due to decreasing K sp value with a pH reduction. As the K sp of Fe(OH)3 is much smaller than the Cu(OH)2 ((Fe(OH)3 = 2.8 × 10−39, Cu(OH)2 = 2.2 × 10−20), it is easier to form Fe(OH)3 in acidic media and induce N-CDs aggregation [34]. It is shown that the N-CDs have suitable selectivity for Fe3+ ions due to superior affinity between the Fe3+ ions and the numerous hydroxyl, carboxyl, and amino groups on the N-CDs surface. The half-filled 3d orbital of Fe3+ ions and their unique interplay affect the N-CDs selectivity and sensitivity toward Fe3+ against other metal ions [28]. Therefore, the PL spectra of N-CDs after the addition of Fe3+ in the acidic conditions were recorded. As depicted in (Fig. 5 a), the fluorescence intensity emission at 535 nm gradually decreases with the increase in the amount of Fe3+ in the range of 0–8 μM. There is a linear relationship between the intensity of fluorescence and the concentrations of Fe3+ (Fig. 5b). The linear equation states as below:
(2)
F o
F
= 0.2299 X + 1.0352

F0 and F are the PL intensity of N-CDs in the absence and the presence of Fe3+, respectively, and X indicates the concentration of Fe3+ ions. The LOD was measured based on 3
δ
/m (
δ
is the standard deviation of blank and m is the slope of the calibration curve) [35–37]. From equation (2), according to the standard deviation of blank (0.0010635) and slop of calibration curve (0.2299), the LOD was detected to be 13.8 nM. This LOD is acceptable and less than the maximum level (0.54 μM) of Fe3+ ion permissive in drinking water by the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency. The optical and analytical parameters were comparable to or even lower than those previously reported in (Table 2 ), which was used to detect Fe3+ ions. Although our proposed sensor works in the off-state, It was found that synthesized N-CDs in this work exhibited a sensitive and selective response for measuring Fe3+ ions in an aqueous solution due to the selection of a suitable precursor and the presence of heteroatoms such as nitrogen. Unlike other compounds, the aforementioned synthesis N-CDs do not require severe synthetic conditions, complex post-treatment methods, strong solvents, and surface passivation reagents. However, the N-CDs prepared in this work have been used specifically to measure Fe3+ ions in the presence of 19 other metal ions.

CDs have various functional groups and optical properties that may link targets through separate mechanisms. Typically, CDs fluorescence quenching is a result of static quenching or dynamic quenching. In this work, the possible mechanism has been studied through Zeta potential, UV–vis absorption, and temperature-dependent studies. The best way to describe the fluorescence quenching mechanism of N-CDs follows by the Stern-Volmer equation [38]:
(3)
F o
F
= 1 + K s v
[ Q ]
Where F
0 and F are the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs in the absence and presence of Fe3+, [Q] is the concentration of the quencher (Fe3+), K SV is the static Stern–Volmer quenching constant. The fluorescence quenching tests were carried out at three different temperatures, 298, 303, and 308 K, and K SV was measured from the slope of the linear regression of the F0/F plot versus [Q] as reported in equation (3). The Stern–Volmer figures are presented in (Fig. 6 a), and the results are summarized in (Table 3 ). Based on (Table 3), the K SV values for the quenching of N-CDs by Fe3+ ions were decreased as the temperature increased. Consequently, it can be concluded that the N-CDs fluorescence quenching by Fe3+ is most likely caused by a static quenching mechanism resulting from the formation of a ground-state complex. The quenching interaction between N-CDs through Fe3+ ions may be ascribed to the efficiency of coordination or chelation interactions between Fe3+ ions and various functional groups on the surface of N-CDs.

To further study the type of quenching mechanism, the Zeta potential measurements of N-CDs were carried out to determine if the Fe3+ coordinates with oxygen or amino groups on the N-CDs surface. As displayed in (Fig. 6b), the Zeta potential value changed from −10.0 mV to −18.32 mV after adding quencher. That was assigned to the coordination of amino groups on the surface of N-CDs with Fe3+ and the formation of the ground-state complex, so the positive charge of N-CDs is protected with Fe3+ ions [13].

Finally, the UV–vis absorption spectra of N-CDs, N-CDs/Fe3+, and Fe3+ are used to confirm N-CDs static quenching mechanism. As indicated in (Fig. 6c), when Fe3+ is added to the as-prepared N-CDs, the absorption peak centered at 355 nm appears, and the intensity of the peak at 290 nm increased. Also, the UV–vis spectrum of N-CDs/Fe3+ is different from the absorption peak of Fe3+ and shifted towards a longer wavelength indicating the formation of the ground-state complex and static quenching mechanism of N-CDs [13].

To indicate the validity of the present fluorescence N-CDs probe, different concentrations of Fe3+ ions in the range from 0.5 μM to 5 μM were injected into solutions including tap or mineral water and N-CDs. Then emission spectra for each sample were recorded. The results are shown in (Table 4 ), indicating that the fluorescence probe had satisfying recoveries in an acceptable range from 97 to 106.4% and RSD in the range of 1.88–4.01%. These results showed that the proposed method offers enough reliability and sensitivity to detect Fe3+ ions in real samples [39].

To elucidate the synthesized N-CDs biocompatibility, the cell viability of as-prepared N-CDs was measured in HeLa cells using MTT assay. The Cells were treated with various concentrations of N-CDs from 0 mg mL−1 to 1.4 mg mL−1 for 24 h. As indicated in (Fig. 7 ), the viability of HeLa cells was more than 91% when the N-CDs concentrations were lower than 1.4 mg mL−1, validating low cytotoxicity after 24 h of treatment with N-CDs. Accordingly, it can be suggested as an excellent biocompatible label for in vitro and in vivo applications [40,41].

In this work, the N-CDs have been synthesized via a one-pot hydrothermal treatment. The obtained N-CDs with desirable QY (30.2%) were developed to detect Fe3+ ions based on fluorescence ON-OFF. Fe3+ ions could quench the designed fluorescent probe with acceptable LOD in acidic conditions owning to the lower K sp value of the metal hydroxide at the lower pH. Fe3+ ions have much affinity towards hydroxyl, carboxyl, and amino groups on the surface of N-CDs and form a ground-state complex through a static quenching mechanism. This probe with acceptable recoveries ranging from 97 to 106.4% is sensitive and selective for detecting Fe3+ ions in real complex samples. Besides, the synthesized N-CDs have several distinct advantages, including the significant solubility in water, excellent stability, low cytotoxicity, low cost, reasonable sensitivity, and acceptable selectivity compared to other methods. Therefore, the reported N-CDs have favorable applications in detecting Fe3+ ions in real water samples.

Nazanin Hashemi: Conceptualization, Methodology, Validation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Resources, Writing – original draft, and, Writing – review & editing, and, Project administration. Mohammad H. Mousazadeh: Supervision, and, Project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are interested in acknowledging Amirkabir University of Technology (Tehran, Iran) for this work's financial support.
